summary: Karin Svenson Berg was my great-grandmother on my mother's side, and she was a remarkable woman educated at the University of Lund in Sweden. In the late 1800s, she was a schoolteacher in her hometown of Osby and planned to marry Andrew Berg, who had immigrated to America. After Andrew died in Minnesota, she married his brother Nils and settled in Wahkon, Minnesota. I have many treasured items that belonged to her, including a basket box she carried from Sweden and gifts from her schoolchildren in 1902. Her story is a cherished part of my family history and immigrant heritage.

My great-grandmother, Karin Svenson Berg, was educated at the University of Lund in Sweden. This was in the late 1800s, and after her education there, she became a schoolteacher in her hometown of Osby. The university played a key role in shaping her early career before she emigrated to America. Her education was something my family has always respected and remembered.

Osby is the small town in Sweden where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg grew up and taught school. It was there in Osby that she was engaged to Andrew Berg before he immigrated to America. Both Andrew and his brother Nils came from Osby, and it was the starting point of their family's journey to Minnesota. Osby holds a special place in our family's history as the origin of their immigrant story.

Andrew Berg was my great-grandmother Karin's first love and fiancé from Osby, Sweden. He had immigrated to America before her, but sadly he died in Minnesota before Karin could join him. His brother Nils found their correspondence and later married Karin, which is how our family came to be. I still have a tiny box with Andrew's picture that Karin kept, showing she never forgot him.

Nils Berg was Andrew Berg’s brother who lived in Minnesota and played a pivotal role in my family’s history. After Andrew died, Nils found the letters between Andrew and Karin and wrote to her, inviting her to come to America and marry him. Karin agreed, and they married at the Kanabec County Courthouse before settling in Wahkon, Minnesota. Nils and Karin were my great-grandparents, and their story is truly amazing considering they had never met before.

Minnesota is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg came after leaving Sweden in 1902. It’s where Andrew Berg had immigrated and where Nils Berg lived, and where Karin eventually settled after marrying Nils. Places like Mora, Wahkon, and the Kanabec County Courthouse are all in Minnesota and are central to my family’s immigrant story. Minnesota became home to my ancestors and the place where my grandfather Arthur Berg was born.

Mora, Minnesota is the town where my great-grandmother Karin arrived by train after traveling from Sweden. Nils Berg met her at the train depot there, and from Mora they traveled on horseback to their home in Wahkon. Mora was an important arrival point in their journey and a key location in their early days in America.

The Kanabec County Courthouse is where my great-grandparents Karin and Nils Berg were married after she arrived in America. This courthouse is located near Mora, Minnesota, and it marks the official beginning of their married life together. It’s a significant place in my family’s history, symbolizing their union and new start in the United States.

Wahkon, Minnesota is where my great-grandparents Karin and Nils Berg lived after their marriage. It’s about 30 miles northwest of Mora, and they traveled there on horseback when Karin first arrived. Wahkon became their lifelong home and the place where my grandfather Arthur Berg was born in 1903. It holds a special place in our family story as the heart of their American life.

Arthur Berg was the only child of my great-grandparents Karin and Nils Berg, born in Wahkon, Minnesota in 1903. He is my grandfather, and through him our family’s story continued in America. Arthur’s birth marked the beginning of our family’s American generations, rooted in the immigrant journey of Karin and Nils.
